natural_sources: Secretion of female lac insect (Kerria lacca, Laccifer lacca)
natural_occurrence.description: A natural resin secreted by the female lac insect on trees in India and Thailand. The insects secrete the resin to form a protective cocoon. Refined shellac is used as a glazing agent in foods and as a finish for wood furniture.

manufacturing.description: Harvested from tree branches where lac insects have created resinous deposits. The raw material (sticklac) is processed by crushing, washing, filtering, and refining to produce purified shellac flakes or liquid.

other_uses.industrial: Furniture polish, wood finish, vinyl record coating
other_uses.household: French polish for wood, primer/sealer, artist fixative

eu.conditions: Authorized as glazing agent for surface treatment

usa.notes: Approved as resinous glaze on confectionery

canada.reference: Lists of Permitted Glazing and Polishing Agents

safety_evaluations.efsa_adi: Not specified (no safety concern at current use levels)

regulatory_history.event: Shellac used for thousands of years in India for wood finishing and lacquerware
FDA affirms shellac as GRAS for food use
EFSA confirms safety as food additive

commonly_found_in: Confectioner's glaze on donuts and pastries

adi_context.source_note: No ADI established - shellac is considered safe at all levels of consumption due to its inert nature and lack of absorption.

fun_facts: Made from bug secretions - the same as furniture polish and wood finish
Female lac insects produce the resin to protect their eggs
The shiny coating on jelly beans is literally insect secretion
Used to make old vinyl records before plastics were invented
Not vegan - derived from insect secretions
The word 'shellac' also means 'to defeat decisively' in slang
Takes about 300,000 lac insects to produce 1 kg of shellac
